title: Evaluation of skeletal age based on Greulich-Pyle method in Tehran
author: Ebrahimzade, Rahban, et al.

steps.title: Protocol data
steps.instruction: ‚Ä¢The data demonstrate that variations in skeletal growth in children aged 6‚Äì9.99 and 10‚Äì13.99 years old are greater than those evaluated by the Greulich and Pyle atlas, and that there are significant differences between bone and chronological ages.‚Ä¢The categorizing groups based on BMD shows a great differences among our Mean‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØSD chronological and bone ages in those male subjects who are underweight (BMD‚ÄØ<‚ÄØ18.5) and obese (BMI‚ÄØ‚â•‚ÄØ30). The Mean‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØSD of difference of bone and chronological ages in male normal children (18.5‚ÄØ‚â§‚ÄØBMI‚ÄØ‚â§‚ÄØ24.9) and overweight children (25‚ÄØ‚â§‚ÄØBMI‚ÄØ‚â§‚ÄØ29.9) were 0.14‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØ0.55 and 0.87‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØ0.41 years, respectively.‚Ä¢The Mean‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØSD difference of chronological and bone ages in female subjects that are underweight, normal, and obese is ‚àí0.40‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØ2.69, 0.50‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØ0.49 and 0.45‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØ0.63, respectively.‚Ä¢The variation in skeletal growth of Iranian children and children‚Äôs group that the GP Atlas was taken could be related to environmental, economic, social, cultural and racial differences. Although this discrepancy is greater in boys than girls, so it may be affected by gender in Iran. Thus, GP Atlas could be more accurate to determine bone age in Iranian girls than boys.

steps.title: Description of protocol
steps.instruction: The Mean‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØSD bone and chronological ages, weight, height and BMI of subjects based on gender are presented. Although the Mean‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØSD BMI in boys was more than girls, the difference between Mean‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØSD chronological and bone age in boys was lower than that of girls. The difference of Mean‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØSD of chronological and bone ages in group 1 was more than other aged grouped (Table 1, Fig. 1). Also, the highest Mean‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØSD BMI was observed in group 3 (Table 2). The number of male subjects and Mean‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØSD chronological and bone ages according to BMI ranges are shown in Table 3. In the BMI‚ÄØ<‚ÄØ18.5, 18.5‚ÄØ‚â§‚ÄØBMI‚ÄØ‚â§‚ÄØ24.9, 25‚ÄØ‚â§‚ÄØBMI‚ÄØ‚â§‚ÄØ29.9 and BMI‚ÄØ‚â•‚ÄØ30, Mean‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØSD bone ages in boys were ‚àí0.40‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØ2.69, 0.50‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØ0.49, 0.45‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØ0.63, 4.05‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØ0.70 years delayed than the Mean‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØSD chronological ages, respectively. Also, Mean‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØSD chronological and bone ages in girls according to BMI ranges are shown in Table 4. In the BMI‚ÄØ<‚ÄØ18.5, 18.5‚ÄØ‚â§‚ÄØBMI‚ÄØ‚â§‚ÄØ24.9 and BMI‚ÄØ‚â•‚ÄØ30 groups of girls, Mean‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØSD bone ages were ‚Äì0.40‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØ2.69, 0.50‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØ0.49, 0.45‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØ0.63 years delayed than the Mean‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØSD chronological ages, respectively. steps.title: Materials and methods
steps.instruction: The aim of this methodology is to determine the differences between bone age and chronological age in Iranian children which could lead more accurate estimation of bone age. To compare bone age with chronological age, 40 children were randomly selected. Subjects aged between 2 and 18 years living in Tehran City, Iran. Exclusion criteria were history of systemic diseases more than 1 month, history of chronic systemic diseases, history of hospitalization for more than a week, and people with height or weight above the percentile of 97% or below 3 percent percentile. After receiving informed consent from patients and their parents, radiograph of the left wrist and hand was taken. Based on GP [5], all radiographs were reported by a radiologist who was of the patient‚Äôs chronological age. These individuals were divided into four groups: 2‚Äì5.99, 6‚Äì9.99, 10‚Äì13.99 and 14‚Äì18 years old.

steps.title: Statistical analysis
steps.instruction: The normality of data was checked out by Shapiro‚ÄìWilk Test. For each group, mean‚ÄØ¬±‚ÄØSD of bone age, chronological age, weight, height and BMI (Body Mass Index) were determined; then data were analyzed using One-Sample T-Test by SPSS.
